name: Hash Set (one pass)
summary: Scan once, keeping a hash table of values already seen (a set); the moment a value is already present, a duplicate exists. O(1) average membership check per element.
visualizationIntent: init: an empty hash set is shown. loop: we take the next value num from the array. check: num flies to the set as the lookup key — if an entry with that value already exists it flashes (compared), meaning we've seen it before. update: a brand-new value is inserted into the set (pop-in). done: on a hit, the matching entry glows result (green) — a duplicate is found and we return true. return: if the scan finishes with no hit, every value was distinct and we return false.

lineExplanations.3: Create an empty hash table used as a set of values seen so far.
lineExplanations.4: Take the next value from the array to test.
lineExplanations.5: Check in O(1) whether this value is already in the set.
lineExplanations.6: It is — a repeat exists, so return true immediately.
lineExplanations.7: It's new — record it in the set and keep scanning.
lineExplanations.8: The whole array was scanned with no repeat, so return false.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def containsDuplicate(self, nums): — defines the method; nums is the integer array.
syntaxExplanations.3: seen = {} — an empty dict used as a set of values seen so far.
syntaxExplanations.4: for num in nums: — iterate over each value in the array in order.
syntaxExplanations.5: if num in seen: — O(1) average membership check: is this value already recorded?
syntaxExplanations.6: return True — a repeat exists, so report a duplicate immediately.
syntaxExplanations.7: seen[num] = True — record this value as seen (True is just a presence marker).
syntaxExplanations.8: return False — the loop finished with no repeat, so all values are distinct.
